Lesson 1 of 3

ML pipeline design

Data pipelines, feature stores, training infrastructure, and experiment tracking for reproducible ML development.

30-40 min AI/ML flow MLOps and production AI 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Production ML requires reproducible pipelines. Ad-hoc notebooks do not scale to teams or production reliability requirements.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Data pipelines Data validation (Great Expectations, TFX) catches data quality issues before training2. Training infrastructure Experiment tracking (MLflow, W&B) records parameters, metrics, and artifacts3. Pipeline orchestration DAG-based orchestration handles complex dependencies between pipeline stages

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Production ML requires reproducible pipelines. Ad-hoc notebooks do not scale to teams or production reliability requirements. 2. Data pipelines Data validation (Great Expectations, TFX) catches data quality issues before training3. Training infrastructure Experiment tracking (MLflow, W&B) records parameters, metrics, and artifacts4. Pipeline orchestration DAG-based orchestration handles complex dependencies between pipeline stages
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Production ML requires reproducible pipelines. Ad-hoc notebooks do not scale to teams or production reliability requirements.

What to say first

  • Can design a reproducible training pipeline with proper versioning.
  • Understands feature store benefits for training-serving consistency.
  • Knows how to orchestrate multi-stage ML workflows.
2

Study section

Data pipelines

ETL, data validation, feature computation, and versioning ensure models train on clean, reproducible datasets.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Data validation (Great Expectations, TFX) catches data quality issues before training
  • Feature stores (Feast, Tecton) enable feature reuse and consistency between training and serving
  • Data versioning (DVC, LakeFS) enables reproducibility and rollback
3

Study section

Training infrastructure

Distributed training, GPU management, hyperparameter optimization, and experiment tracking at scale.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Experiment tracking (MLflow, W&B) records parameters, metrics, and artifacts
  • Distributed training (DeepSpeed, FSDP) enables training models that exceed single-GPU memory
  • Hyperparameter optimization (Optuna, Ray Tune) automates search over configuration space

Code example

Track experiments with MLflow

import mlflow
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

X, y = load_iris(return_X_y=True)

mlflow.set_experiment("iris-classification")

# Log each hyperparameter combination as a run
for n_estimators in [50, 100, 200]:
    for max_depth in [3, 5, None]:
        with mlflow.start_run():
            model = RandomForestClassifier(
                n_estimators=n_estimators, max_depth=max_depth
            )
            scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=5)

            # Log params and metrics
            mlflow.log_params({"n_estimators": n_estimators, "max_depth": max_depth})
            mlflow.log_metric("accuracy_mean", scores.mean())
            mlflow.log_metric("accuracy_std", scores.std())

            # Log the trained model artifact
            model.fit(X, y)
            mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, "model")

# Compare runs: mlflow ui --port 5000
4

Study section

Pipeline orchestration

Airflow, Kubeflow Pipelines, and Prefect coordinate multi-stage ML workflows with dependency management.

Call these points out explicitly

  • DAG-based orchestration handles complex dependencies between pipeline stages
  • Retry logic and checkpointing prevent full restarts on transient failures
  • Scheduling and triggers enable automated retraining on data freshness signals

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Can design a reproducible training pipeline with proper versioning.
  • Understands feature store benefits for training-serving consistency.
  • Knows how to orchestrate multi-stage ML workflows.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Running production training from Jupyter notebooks without version control.
  • Not versioning data alongside code and model artifacts.
  • Building monolithic pipelines that are hard to debug and iterate on.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • Design an ML pipeline for a recommendation system that retrains daily.
  • How would you ensure training-serving skew does not degrade model quality?
  • Compare Kubeflow Pipelines and Airflow for ML orchestration. When would you choose each?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

1 code example 1 design drill

What to master

Study this in your own words

  • Can design a reproducible training pipeline with proper versioning.
  • Understands feature store benefits for training-serving consistency.
  • Knows how to orchestrate multi-stage ML workflows.

Production lens

Failure modes to call out

  • Running production training from Jupyter notebooks without version control.
  • Not versioning data alongside code and model artifacts.
  • Building monolithic pipelines that are hard to debug and iterate on.

Practice aloud

Questions worth rehearsing

  • Design an ML pipeline for a recommendation system that retrains daily.
  • How would you ensure training-serving skew does not degrade model quality?
  • Compare Kubeflow Pipelines and Airflow for ML orchestration. When would you choose each?

Code example

Implement a minimal feature store

advanced coding

Build a simplified feature store that serves features consistently for both training (batch) and serving (online) with point-in-time correctness.

What to notice

  • Online serving always returns the latest feature values
  • Historical queries must respect point-in-time: only return features that existed BEFORE the query timestamp
  • This prevents data leakage in training

Expected outcome

Online returns latest (52.0, 15); Historical March returns Jan values (45.0, 10)

Starter code 
from typing import Dict, List, Any
from datetime import datetime
import json

class FeatureStore:
    def __init__(self):
        self.features: Dict[str, List[Dict]] = {}  # entity_id -> [{timestamp, features}]

    def ingest(self, entity_id: str, features: Dict[str, Any], timestamp: datetime):
        """Store a feature vector with timestamp."""
        # TODO
        pass

    def get_online(self, entity_id: str) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        """Get latest features for real-time serving."""
        # TODO
        pass

    def get_historical(self, entity_id: str, as_of: datetime) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        """Get features as they were at a point in time (for training)."""
        # TODO: Return most recent features BEFORE as_of timestamp
        pass

# Test
fs = FeatureStore()
fs.ingest("user_123", {"avg_order": 45.0, "total_orders": 10}, datetime(2024, 1, 1))
fs.ingest("user_123", {"avg_order": 52.0, "total_orders": 15}, datetime(2024, 6, 1))
print("Online:", fs.get_online("user_123"))
print("Historical (March):", fs.get_historical("user_123", datetime(2024, 3, 1)))
Reference solution 
from typing import Dict, List, Any
from datetime import datetime

class FeatureStore:
    def __init__(self):
        self.features = {}

    def ingest(self, entity_id: str, features: Dict[str, Any], timestamp: datetime):
        if entity_id not in self.features:
            self.features[entity_id] = []
        self.features[entity_id].append({"timestamp": timestamp, "features": features})
        self.features[entity_id].sort(key=lambda x: x["timestamp"])

    def get_online(self, entity_id: str) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        if entity_id not in self.features or not self.features[entity_id]:
            return {}
        return self.features[entity_id][-1]["features"]

    def get_historical(self, entity_id: str, as_of: datetime) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        if entity_id not in self.features:
            return {}
        valid = [f for f in self.features[entity_id] if f["timestamp"] <= as_of]
        return valid[-1]["features"] if valid else {}

Design drill

Design an ML training pipeline DAG

intermediate

Design a complete ML pipeline for a recommendation system that retrains daily. Define stages, dependencies, data validation checks, and rollback procedures.

Use these prompts to deepen the study pass

  • What are the stages: data extraction, validation, feature engineering, training, evaluation, deployment?
  • What data validation checks would you add between extraction and training?
  • How do you handle training failure: retry, alert, or fall back to previous model?
  • What triggers retraining: schedule, data drift, or performance degradation?

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later.

0/3 saved Step 1/3: Explain the concept
Ln 1, Col 1 1 lines 0 words 0.0 KB markdown

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Explain the concept

Explain "ML pipeline design" as if teaching a senior engineer who is new to AI/ML. Cover the core intuition, when and why you would use it, and the key algorithms or techniques involved.

Goal

Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Guardrails

  • Design an ML pipeline for a recommendation system that retrains daily.
  • Data pipelines
  • Training infrastructure
  • Can design a reproducible training pipeline with proper versioning.

Suggested Structure

  • Core intuition
  • Key algorithms or techniques
  • When to apply
  • Common variants
